original_abstract: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) represent the leading non-viral platform for gene delivery. While it is well-established that physicochemical properties such as size, surface charge, and surface modifications dictate their performance, the specific impact of these attributes on transfection and distribution following intramuscular injection remains poorly understood. In this study, we synthesized a library of LNPs with varying sizes, lipid-based charges, and PEGylated lipid compositions to evaluate their behavior in vivo, using luciferase mRNA (mLuc) as a reporter. Our findings reveal that smaller, neutrally charged LNPs achieve superior transfection efficiency at the injection site. Interestingly, however, size and charge did not significantly alter the systemic distribution of the nanoparticles. We further demonstrated that PEGylated lipids with shorter acyl chains enhance transfection by facilitating more efficient cellular uptake and endosomal escape. Of note, the underlying mechanisms of cellular internalizations were found to shift depending on the acyl chain length of the PEGylated lipids. Collectively, these results highlight the critical role of nanoparticle morphology and PEG chemistry in optimizing intramuscular mRNA delivery, providing a strategic framework for the development of more potent LNP-based vaccines and therapeutics.
humanized_abstract: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are currently the most common non-viral delivery method used in the field of gene delivery. Even though it is well established that the properties such as size, charge and surface modifications have crucial impacts on their functionality, how these physicochemical characteristics affect the biological behaviors of LNPs post-injection is not fully understood. In our study, a set of NPs with defined size, lipid charge and PEGylated lipid structure were formulated to deliver luciferase mRNA (mLuc) for the first time, to investigate how different NPs characteristics affect gene expression and bio-distribution upon intramuscular injection. We found that smaller, un-charged LNP exhibited higher gene expression in muscle tissues, even though particle size and charge had less effect on NP bio-distribution in vivo. Meanwhile, shorter acyl chain in the PEGylated lipid contributed to better NP functionalities such as improved internalization, endosomal release at the muscle cells, and modulating the mechanisms of different internalizations pathways accordingly. Therefore, Our work here will allow us to clearly understand the detailed requirements for each composition to generate effective intramuscular delivered mRNA for either vaccine or gene therapy applications using LNP.
